column21: I understand the commitment and am certain I can make it.
column23: I am drawn to Generate because it offers a unique opportunity to combine my passion for event planning, marketing, and community-building within an experiential learning environment that empowers students to innovate and collaborate. As someone with experience in coordinating impactful events—from boosting social media engagement at the Lewis Latimer House Museum to leading First-Gen Week at Northeastern—I am excited to bring my creativity and organizational skills to a role that fosters real-world product development while strengthening connections among team members. The Chief of Event role at Generate perfectly aligns with my values of creating inclusive, inspiring spaces where everyone feels supported and celebrated as they grow, learn, and succeed together.
column24: One of the most impact communities been the First Generation Club at Northeastern, where I’ve had the privilege to serve as the Special Events Lead. As a first-generation college student, I understand the challenges of navigating higher education without a built-in network of guidance and support. This community taught me the power of collaboration and inclusion by showing me that growth is most impactful when we lift each other up—through events like financial literacy workshops, community brunches, and open forums where every voice is heard. I learned that meaningful progress happens when diverse perspectives come together, fostering an environment where empathy and resilience thrive. At Generate, I would bring these lessons by ensuring that every event I plan is inclusive, accessible, and designed to celebrate each team member’s unique journey, helping them feel seen, supported, and empowered to grow both personally and professionally.
column25:
Here’s a polished one-paragraph response tailored to Emily Wang’s background and experience, focusing on her approach to leading a team and integrating delegation, morale, and support:

My approach to leading a team centers on creating an environment where every member feels valued and empowered to contribute meaningfully. I prioritize open communication, ensuring that everyone understands our goals and feels comfortable sharing ideas. I believe that the best results come from delegating tasks based on each team member’s skills and interests—matching responsibilities to what excites them while providing opportunities for growth. Building morale is crucial, so I celebrate achievements both big and small, foster an inclusive space where everyone’s voice matters, and stay attentive to each person’s well-being and workload. By balancing structured guidance with flexibility, I aim to cultivate a collaborative team where we learn from each other and succeed together.
column26: One event I’d love to run is a “Creative Jam” workshop in collaboration with Adobe, where students can come together to explore design tools like Photoshop, Illustrator, and Premiere Pro in a fun, low-pressure environment. As an Adobe Ambassador, I could provide free merch, exclusive templates, and hands-on tutorials to spark creativity while also fostering a sense of community. By partnering with Generate’s product teams, we could showcase how design enhances product storytelling and empower members to bring their ideas to life with professional-level resources.

column21: I understand the commitment and am certain I can make it.
column23: There’s so much talent and passion that goes unseen or even undiscovered. That’s why I value an organization like Generate that cultivates a community with passionate and motivated individuals, both as clients and members, so they have the opportunity to gain hands-on experience and grow professionally and socially. Creating a community where there is interdependency and strong potential for growth and experience is a community I hope to be a part of. A community with strong, motivated, like-minded individuals and a goal is unstoppable. I hope to be part of the process where I can foster each member’s experience and contribute to their professional growth to be able to create a stronger Generate community. I hope that members will gain value from being a part of this community by skill-building and networking and being able to use those skills and experiences in their work at Generate and their professional life as well. I’m drawn to this role specifically because I can contribute to bettering the Generate team by supporting each member as a stronger candidate and a Generate member. I hope to be a part of promoting growth and professionalism offering valued skills and experiences only at Generate.
column24: During high school, I founded a project that focuses on advocacy for student mental health. I was able to build a committee of student leaders, faculty members, staff, district members, etc. We worked towards a day dedicated to mental health throughout the school year and providing more mental health resources. This project has become a symbol of mental health advocacy and a new tradition even after I graduated. I still look back to this team and reflect on our success. This diverse team of people with different ages, experiences, and perspectives were able to create an impact to our community. I was just a high school student when I founded this project. I would’ve not been able to understand the logistics behind a big project like this nor have the district support and 30+ sponsors if it wasn’t for the support and collaboration of my team. When mental health was a sensitive and controversial topic at my school, these different backgrounds did spark some disagreement at times, but we were working towards a collective goal and each person contributed with their unique experiences, skills, and network. This experience was valuable for me because it allowed me to see what a group of people can do when there is a goal and when each person contributes their own strength. I hope to bring this experience into Generate being a person who can reflect on my strengths and weaknesses and other’s strengths to create a community of interdependence and collaboration, and through that, build a strong team with strong outcomes and growing individuals.
column25: I’ve worked with many leader-figures I look up to and what I’ve learned is to be inclusive and open yet firm. I’ve sat through meetings where the leader is too open where we sit in the meeting room for an hour and it ends with “let’s all think about this and meet again tomorrow” and I’ve also been on teams where the leader offered no flexibility in opinion and work styles where I felt like a robot pushing out work. The teams I’ve seen most success with is a leader who holds both traits with a good balance. As a leader, I value my team and their opinions because I know I can’t do it without them, yet, I also have to make sure my team shows efficiency and productivity. During my time as events chair in the Korean American Student Association, I ran multiple committees to plan events. For every meeting, I came prepared with a structured agenda that included essential items we needed to finalize, along with space for open brainstorming. My meetings would always start with asking people about their day or any plans for the weekend. To make sure deadlines were met, I check in to make sure everyone in my team has the resources and support they need to meet those deadlines. At the end of our project/event, I always made sure to celebrate our wins. It could be as small as sending a message after the event thanking everyone for their hard work and to rest up or team bonding where we could put the work burden down and connect as friends. Throughout my time working with different teams, I’ve learned that being able to connect as people has brought the best results and also experience. I’m a leader who can empathize and listen to be able to create an open environment for people to vocalize but also someone who values the performance of the team and stands firm regarding the goal we are trying to reach as a team.
column26: Most of the time, we try to present our successes and although that is important, learning from the failures is also a key skill to gain to be able to grow. One idea I have is failure presentations where people can present their failures in projects or their work so we can come together and reflect on failures and use those lessons to grow. This makes the successes in the team so much more meaningful and allows people to celebrate the small wins and create an atmosphere where people can try rather than fear failure. Many people feel imposter syndrome when they see big projects gathering success but behind those successes, there were the many failures that led up to it and creating a space for people to celebrate their failures can make Generate a more risk-taking community where we don’t hide failures but we reflect on them.

column21: I understand the commitment and am certain I can make it.
column23:
Generate seems like the ideal place to learn, grow, and connect, and that’s what drew me in. As someone who’s fascinated by the world of venture capital and entrepreneurship, I love that Generate captures that same energy and strategic thinking. Looking at all the projects on Generate’s website, I was struck by how unique each one was. It feels like an environment where I can be constantly inspired by different perspectives and learn about fields and ideas I might never have explored on my own. The opportunity to contribute to impactful products, while also growing as a leader, is something that really draws me in. I value real-world experience that feels meaningful and collaborative. I liked that Generate incorporates people from different backgrounds and majors, as well as fosters a community. I don’t want to focus solely on technical skills for example. Besides team meetings, I love that club members have different ways of connecting and having fun. As a Global Scholar who didn’t have access to a consistent range of clubs in my first year, it’s important to me to join clubs that combine learning with genuine community.

What really excites me about the Chief of Events role is how it brings together my passion for creative collaboration and my love for planning. I’ve always found so much joy in organizing, whether it’s mapping out a vacation or putting together a detailed schedule. In my professional roles in the past, I’ve had the chance to orchestrate meaningful experiences. As a Creative Assistant for Northeastern Oakland Student Life, I helped prepare buildings for campus events, while as VP of Marketing for the Teen Board, I worked closely with the leadership committee to plan each second of our board meetings. These specific duties from my roles were some of the most important to me. At Generate, I see an incredible opportunity to channel that passion into building a community where people feel supported, energized, and ready to create their best work. I’m also excited by the idea of leading a team that shares the same devotion I do.
column24:
Being a reader has always been a way to connect with people and build community. Reading books opened my mind to new ideas, different experiences, and perspectives I might never have encountered otherwise. That’s why one of the most formative communities I’ve been a part of has been the book clubs I helped build. At our high school, reading wasn’t very popular. I’d hear people talk about how boring it was or how they’d never pick up a book unless they had to for class. But my friend and I were determined to change that. We wanted to create a space where people could share stories, discover new genres, and build friendships around books.

We started by asking our classmates what kinds of books they might actually be interested in and used those suggestions to choose our first reads. We brought snacks to meetings and made them feel like a fun, welcoming place rather than another assignment. Slowly, our club grew from just the two of us to 26 members, which was a huge deal at our small private school.

Along the way, I learned so much about leadership. Collaboration is a key part of maintaining a successful club. We had to coordinate with teachers to reserve space and work with the school administration to organize activities like our first book drive. Working with my friend to run the club also taught me how to effectively, share responsibilities and support each other’s ideas. Making sure everyone was benefitting from this experience was the only way we’d keep all our members and get more to join. I saw firsthand how small details, like choosing books that reflected members’ interests, made all the difference. 

At Generate, I’d bring those lessons with me. Whether it’s planning a team event or just brainstorming ideas, I’d bring that same spirit of community-building that I learned from the book club. Collaboration means listening and adapting. Inclusion means creating space for everyone’s ideas. Growth happens when people feel supported and inspired to participate.
column25:
Leadership to me is not about being in absolute control. My approach to leading a team is all about creating a supportive, collaborative environment where everyone feels empowered to do their best work. I see my role as removing roadblocks so that the people around me can shine. I believe in letting my team have ownership over their tasks, while still providing good oversight and coaching. Regular one-on-one meetings are a key part of how I lead. I use them to provide feedback, check in on progress, and offer support. Ideas should be a collective effort, not just something that comes from the top down. It’s important to prompt team members to share their ideas and encourage everyone to contribute to brainstorming sessions. If I do come up with an idea myself, I’ll ensure everyone is on board before moving forward. “Being in charge” means being in charge of support. 

Being a good leader requires many skills and attributes. Vision means having a clear picture of where the team is headed and helping each person see how their contributions fit into that journey. Leaders should always try to connect people’s daily tasks and individual values to the larger goals of the team or organization. This helps people find meaning in their work and builds a sense of purpose and trust. Courage encourages both team members and leaders to take bold actions that move things in the right direction. It can be hard to speak up sometimes, whether you want to voice a new idea or provide feedback, but a leader needs to. 

When it comes to delegation, I recognize that I’m not the best at everything, and that’s okay. I follow John Maxwell’s 80% rule: I give tasks to team members who can perform them at least 80% as well as I could. This not only empowers them to develop their skills but also frees me up to focus on the bigger picture. To build morale, I prioritize giving everyone a voice in decisions that affect them. I make sure to provide clarity about where we’re going and why, and I always celebrate milestones. I encourage work-life balance, too, because I believe that supporting the team as whole people is key to their success and happiness.
column26:
I’d love to organize a brainstorming brunch. This would be a laid-back morning meetup where members can grab breakfast together and then break into small, cross-disciplinary groups to come up with or prototype ideas. It would be a casual, collaborative space. 

I think this could also be a great way to engage alumni. Each brunch could have a guest speaker or mentor who’s a Generate alum share their journey and insights.

column23:
I haven’t been part of Generate yet, but I’ve been drawn to it ever since I heard about how it brings together creative, ambitious students to work on real projects. What stands out to me is how hands-on it is. I love that people are trusted to lead and learn by doing. I’ve worked with startups and been part of IDEA at Northeastern, and I’ve seen how impactful it is when you give people ownership and support.

The Chief of Alumni Relations role really excites me because I love connecting with people and hearing their stories. I see this as a chance to build relationships that don’t just end after someone graduates. I want to help alumni feel like they’re still part of the Generate community and show current members that the impact they make here can last well beyond Northeastern.
column24:
Mental Milestones, a mental health platform I co-founded, completely shaped how I work with people. We ran global webinars, collaborated with professionals, and built a space where participants felt supported and safe. That experience taught me that the best teams are the ones where every voice is respected and people feel like they belong. It also taught me how to take initiative and build something from the ground up.

I’d bring those lessons to Generate by making sure every alumni interaction feels meaningful and intentional. Whether it’s organizing events, asking for feedback, or just sending a check-in email, I’d aim to create a culture where people feel like they matter and want to stay involved.
column25:
I lead with empathy, structure, and trust. I think good leadership is about setting clear goals, giving people space to do what they’re good at, and showing up when things get tough. When I led branding for IDEA, I worked with a team that had different strengths and styles, so I focused on strong communication and being open to new ideas while keeping everyone aligned on the bigger picture.

I delegate based on what people are excited about or want to grow in, not just what needs to get done. To keep morale high, I always make sure people feel appreciated and supported. I believe in building a team culture where people can learn, take risks, and actually enjoy the process.

column23:
It’s one of the few spaces on campus that really seems to value people and process just as much as product. I’m someone who loves building structure around people like leading brand strategy at IDEA  and I’ve always gravitated toward roles where I can help others feel like they belong, even when they’re figuring things out as they go.

I’m interested in the Chief of Learning & Development role because I’ve always been the kind of person who enjoys making things easier for other people , not by simplifying the work, but by helping them feel confident doing it. Whether that’s onboarding new team members, helping people find their place in a group, or just checking in when someone feels stuck, I love the behind-the-scenes parts of building a good team. This feels like a role that’s less about the spotlight and more about making sure everyone else shines and that’s exactly the kind of work I care about.
column24:
One of the most defining communities I’ve been part of is Mental Milestones, a mental health platform I co-founded in high school. It started with a simple goal. We wanted to make conversations around mental health more open, especially for students who didn’t always have access to support or even the language to ask for it.

We had no funding, no formal structure, just a small team that cared deeply. We organized webinars with professionals, created resources, and reached over 2,000 people. But it wasn’t about the numbers. It was about the people who messaged us saying things like “I really needed this today.” That’s when it felt real.

What I learned from that experience is that collaboration is about trust. It’s about showing up for your team even when you’re all learning as you go. Inclusion is not just about having diverse voices, but making sure those voices are heard, supported, and represented in the work you do.

Since then, I’ve tried to carry those lessons into every space I enter. Whether it’s in a leadership role or just being part of a group, I try to create environments where people feel safe asking questions, where every idea is taken seriously, and where no one feels like they have to prove they belong. That’s the kind of community I’d want to help build at Generate.
column25:
I’ve always seen leadership as more than just getting things done. It’s about setting a standard for how people treat one another, how they grow, and what kind of space they’re working in. The leaders who made the biggest impact on me weren’t necessarily the most experienced. They were the ones who genuinely cared, who made room for others to speak up, and who made it okay to not have all the answers right away.

That’s the kind of leader I try to be. I care a lot about creating a team culture where people feel safe to ask questions and confident to take initiative. I like to ask people what they want to learn and make sure they’re given chances to try things outside their comfort zone. Delegation, for me, isn’t just about dividing work. It’s about giving people ownership and the opportunity to grow.

At the core of my approach is a focus on values. I try to lead with honesty, consistency, and kindness. I want my team to feel like progress is just as important as results, that learning is part of the process, and that no one has to prove themselves to earn a place. column21: I understand the commitment and am certain I can make it.
column23: College has shown me that life is full of uncertainty—and viewing that uncertainty as an opportunity is one of the most important skills I’ve developed. During my time at Northeastern, I’ve had the chance to attend demo days and listen to pitches and network with young founders. While my career path isn’t centered on entrepreneurship, the mindset of the founders I met deeply inspired me. They helped me recognize the potential I hold, the opportunities available to me, and how to make a lasting impact. As college students, our greatest assets are time and opportunity. I used to feel a constant sense of urgency—like I wasn’t doing enough—which led me to become an anxious over-achiever. Seeing how these young entrepreneurs navigated uncertainty changed how I view myself and the time I have. That mindset shift is something I hope everyone can experience. As Chief of Events, I want to help Generate reach more people— not just for aspiring entrepreneurs, but for any student trying to find direction and purpose. I hope to branch Generate out to the community so we can reach people not only who need a community like us, but even those who didn’t know they needed a community like us. I got the courage to apply to Generate because one friend who is involved in Generate told me, “Generate has something for everyone.” I hope I can spread the courage I received to a larger community and be a part of allowing someone to feel a sense of belonging, passion, and direction.
column24: I briefly worked for the Center of Student Involvement (CSI) at Northeastern as an administrative assistant. My supervisor did a wonderful job making sure I was included in any work they were doing and making sure there was collaboration amongst the assistants. Whenever food was catered in the office, a birthday party for an employee, or a new project going on, they always made sure I was a part of it. I was only an administrative assistant who showed up a couple times a week, yet, I felt a part of the team as if I was always there. This allowed me to be more present at the workplace, ask questions without the fear of judgement, and try my best for every task that was given, even if it was small. It opened my eyes to how a healthy team functions and how a team can make someone grow, even in a small position. I carry this experience with me when I join a new team and especially when I lead a team because I’ve experienced how collaboration and inclusion can lead to growth and belonging in a community which impacts teamwork, work quality, employee satisfaction, etc. I hope I can be someone who can create a welcoming, inclusive environment– being that person to ask everyone in the room about their day– as I join Generate hoping that whoever steps foot in the community feels a sense of belonging and growth.

column26: Coming to Northeastern opened my eyes to the vast career opportunities and skills. Looking back at my time in high school, I had limited exposure to the diverse career paths available. When applying to college, my understanding of career options was largely limited to traditional roles. One idea I’d love to see from Generate is an outreach program aimed at local high schools, introducing students to the many career opportunities and teaching them basic software and hardware skills. Since Generate focuses on innovation, introducing different fields where innovation can be used could provide high school students with early exposure to emerging career paths they may not have considered. Through workshops, guest speakers, or interactive events, Generate could help bridge the knowledge gap and inspire the next generation of innovators. This initiative would not only align with Generate’s mission but also helps students make more informed decisions about their futures—something I wish I had when I was younger.

column24:
One of the most influential communities in shaping who I am has been the cultural and academic groups I’ve been involved with during my undergraduate years, particularly as a Cultural Program Coordinator and a Student Coordinator. These experiences taught me that successful collaboration requires not just planning and execution but also empathy and the ability to genuinely listen to others’ perspectives.

While organizing events, I learned how to balance diverse viewpoints, manage logistics under pressure, and create environments where everyone—from the most enthusiastic participants to the more reserved—felt included and valued. I also developed a deep appreciation for the power of small details in making people feel welcomed and engaged.

At Generate, I would bring these lessons to create inclusive, high-impact events that reflect the diverse talents and interests of our community. Whether it’s through making event spaces more accessible, incorporating feedback into planning, or supporting team members in real-time, I’m committed to building a collaborative culture where every member can grow and contribute meaningfully.
column25:
My approach to leadership is rooted in clarity, trust, and empathy. I believe a great leader sets a clear vision, creates space for everyone to contribute, and actively supports each person’s growth.

To me, the most important leadership traits are communication, accountability, and adaptability. I prioritize open communication so everyone understands their role and feels comfortable sharing ideas or concerns. I also believe in holding myself accountable first—when people see their leader taking initiative and responsibility, it inspires confidence and respect.
column26:
One event idea I’d love to bring to Generate is called "Build & Bond: The Creative Sprint." It would be a fast-paced, one-day team event where members from different pods come together to solve mini challenges—technical, design, or community-based—with limited resources and time. Think hackathon meets improv meets team-building. The goal is not just creative output, but cross-pod connection, collaboration under pressure, and fun.

As for onboarding, I’d implement a “First 15 Days” buddy system, pairing new members with experienced ones from outside their pod. It creates early cross-team bonds, makes asking questions less intimidating, and gives new members a broader view of Generate from the start.

To connect with alumni, I’d host "Flashback Fridays"—a casual virtual series where alumni share quick stories about past projects, what they’re doing now, and what they wish they knew as students. It’s low-pressure, high-impact, and keeps the Generate spirit alive across graduating classes.

These are just sparks, but I’m excited by the idea of creating experiences that energize, connect, and leave a lasting impression.

column21: I understand the commitment and am certain I can make it.
column23: I am eager to join Generate because of its community-driven approach to product design and development. I’ve always been fascinated by the product development lifecycle and how entrepreneurial spirit brings the vision to life. Through my leadership experiences with the Entrepreneurs Club and Scout, I’ve seen how engaging events and collaborative environments can empower people to achieve their entrepreneurial goals. As an Associate Director of Operations in E-Club and Senior External Events Coordinator in Scout, I’ve loved creating experiences for the community that encourage them to push boundaries in their ideas. Generate stands out to me because of its commitment to not just the development of its products, but also to its people. I am excited about the Chief of L&D role because I want to use my leadership, organizational, and operational skills to create learning experiences and initiatives that help the community grow. Whether it’s improving onboarding or company outreach processes, I look forward to collaborating with a team that is also passionate about community growth and development. In terms of professional experiences, last summer I gained experience as the Operations intern at The Community Preservation Corporation (CPC) with building and managing a CRM system in HubSpot. I managed client contacts in the system, making sure their contact information and data was accurate and organized. I’ve also taken coursework in Management Information Systems and Databases to further strengthen my skills in building CRMS and data management. I’m excited to bring these technical and organizational skills to support the growth of the Generate community.
column24: One of the most influential teams in my growth was my team at an NEU hackathon in Oakland. Over the course of 24 hours, I learned how important it was to have a team that collaborates effectively and adapts to unforeseen challenges. In such a fast paced environment we became comfortable with getting scrappy, making team decisions quickly, and adjusting our ideas along the way. What made this experience so impactful was the way we leaned into each other’s strengths. Each team member brough something different to the table, and our project’s success came from trusting each other and being open-minded to all the ideas. I saw how inclusion and trust drive effective teamwork and allow the team to be agile and overcome hurdles. That experience taught me how to build strong teams that are able to stay focused on the big picture without getting lost in the weeds. In high-pressure environments it’s easy to be overwhelmed by small setbacks and details, but our ability to trust each other made our teamwork shine. I would bring these lessons to Generate by building trust in a team through clear communication and open mindedness. When team members feel safe sharing their ideas and asking questions, your ideas are able to build on each other and create well-rounded solutions.
column25: I approach leading a team through clear communication and by encouraging taking initiative. I want my teammates to feel inspired and confident in their own abilities to achieve shared goals. Communication and responsibility matter a lot to me since they are the foundation of strong relationships that can withstand pressure and unexpected challenges. Effective collaboration isn’t just about dividing tasks, it’s about understanding and complementing each other’s work styles, strengths, and areas for growth. I value an environment where teammates feel empowered to be proactive about their work and feel safe asking questions when something is unclear. That kind of supportive environment leads to more thoughtful solutions in response to challenges. This view on collaboration driving growth shapes how I delegate tasks. I aim to break down large challenges into smaller and more approachable steps. When someone is having a hard time I want them to feel comfortable voicing it since it’s an important step in coming up with a solution. I think leadership is about creating an inclusive space where people know they’ll be supported no matter what. These factors build team morale and allow progress to be achieved.
column26: An onboarding strategy that I would like to implement is creating a personalized information card for every member. Whether it’s on a Notion page or a website, this information card would include not just professional information, but also fun facts, hobbies, and passions. These fun facts might be niche hobbies that wouldn’t normally come up during a quick conversation at orientation or at a large mixer event. Being able to filter through these information cards to find members with similar interests would be a cool addition to this initiative. Reaching out to the first person creates more opportunities to meet others. I think one of the most rewarding parts of student organizations is discovering how much you can learn from the people around you. Sometimes all it takes is a single shared interest to start a conversation that leads to a lasting connection. Adding on to that I would like to have randomized one on one coffee chats between Generate members across different teams. It’s a simple way to encourage connections across teams, especially for newer members who might not yet know others outside their immediate teams. You also get the opportunity to learn what other teams are doing, and how their team practices and ideas can influence your own. It’s ultimately about creating a tight-knit and supportive community where members can create lifelong connections.

column16: I am apart of other clubs such as Woman in Business and Finance & Investment Club. I also am in a sorority and take part in other extracurricular to benefit my major and career.
column21: I understand the commitment and am certain I can make it.
column23: What draws me to Generate is the intentional balance it strikes between innovation and personal growth. I’m inspired by how the community fosters a developmental experience—not just building products, but building people. I’m particularly aligned with Generate’s values of being inclusive, driven, and growth-oriented, and I’m excited by the chance to work with others who share those priorities. The focus on real-world product development and strong team identity makes this more than just a student organization—it feels like a space where I can both contribute meaningfully and be pushed to grow. That’s the kind of environment I actively seek out.
column24:
One of the most formative communities I’ve been part of is my Chi Omega sorority. Being in Chi O taught me the true meaning of collaboration, especially in a group where everyone brings different strengths, personalities, and perspectives. Whether we were planning philanthropy events, supporting each other during recruitment, or just navigating the ups and downs of college life, I learned how important it is to communicate openly, step up when needed, and lean on one another.

Chi Omega also shaped my understanding of inclusion. We strive to create a space where every sister feels seen, heard, and valued—something I now carry with me into every team or group I join. And through our constant focus on personal and collective development, I learned that growth comes from pushing yourself, but also from lifting others up.

At Generate, I would bring this same mindset—making sure everyone feels supported and challenged, and contributing to a team culture where empathy, initiative, and intentionality are always present. I believe great work comes from great communities, and that’s something Chi Omega instilled in me deeply.
column25:
My approach to leading a team is about creating a positive, supportive space where everyone feels heard and motivated. I value being adaptable, intentional, and kind—staying organized, communicating clearly, and checking in often to make sure everyone’s on the same page.

When delegating, I try to understand people’s strengths and interests so they’re doing work they feel confident and excited about. I build morale by recognizing effort, celebrating small wins, and making space for fun and connection, especially during busy times. To me, good leadership is about helping people grow while making sure the team is moving forward together.
column26:
I’d love to create a “Build & Brunch” event—an informal weekend gathering where members across teams work on low-stakes, creative mini-projects together (think rapid prototyping, mock pitch competitions, or UI redesigns for fun concepts) while sharing food and ideas. It would help strengthen cross-branch connections, spark creativity, and remind us why we love building in the first place.

For onboarding, I think a “buddy system” that pairs new members with returning ones—especially across different teams—could help create more natural connections and reduce the initial overwhelm. Adding small goals or challenges they complete together could make it more engaging.

column16: I don’t have any additional commitments at the moment.
column21: I understand the commitment and am certain I can make it.
column23:
I haven’t been a part of Generate before, but I deeply resonate with its vision of building with purpose, and more importantly — growing with people.

If there’s one thing that defines my journey in tech so far, it’s that I’ve never waited for someone to hand me the answers. I started with little — limited exposure, limited confidence — but a very real hunger to learn. I built my path line by line, project by project, sometimes with guidance and sometimes completely alone. That process of self-driven growth shaped who I am — not just as a backend/frontend engineer, but as someone who now deeply believes in enabling others to learn better, faster, and with more joy.

At work, I’ve taken ownership of projects far outside my comfort zone. I’ve led OS upgrades, debugged customer issues, and mentored peers — not because I had to, but because I’ve always believed knowledge grows when shared. And now, stepping into the Northeastern community, I’m excited to bring that same mindset here: helping others find their learning rhythm, their confidence, and their voice.
column24:
One of the most impactful communities that shaped me was the internal engagement team I was part of during my time at IBM. It wasn’t a technical team, in fact, it was the opposite. We were a group of people from different domains who came together to organize events, mentor juniors, and create space for conversations beyond deadlines and deliverables.

What I learned there was that real collaboration doesn’t begin with skills, it begins with listening. It taught me that people feel most included when they feel understood. That even the most capable teammates can hold back if the environment isn’t right. And that leadership isn't about having all the answers, it’s about making sure everyone else feels confident enough to share theirs.

I carried those lessons into my tech teams too, creating onboarding guides, being the quiet go-to when someone was stuck but didn’t want to ask out loud, and taking initiative to make knowledge-sharing a habit.

At Generate, I want to bring that same energy. Whether I’m in a technical or community role, I want to help shape a culture where people show up not just for the work, but for each other. Where we grow, not in isolation, but by lifting as we climb.
column25:
My leadership approach centers on one key belief: everyone deserves to feel included and valued. Early in my career, I faced challenges as a newcomer among more experienced colleagues. Despite working hard, my efforts often went unnoticed or were overshadowed. That experience showed me how important empathy and support are in leadership.

I prioritize empathy, communication, and trust as the core traits of effective leadership. I lead by listening carefully, understanding individual needs, and creating an environment where team members feel safe to express themselves and take risks. When delegating, I ensure that each person understands the purpose behind their tasks and feels ownership over their work.

Building morale means more than just recognition - it’s about consistently encouraging growth, being present in moments of struggle, and fostering collaboration where everyone’s contribution matters. At the end of the day, we’re not robots executing tasks; we’re humans building something meaningful - together.
column26:
Here’s something I’d love to bring to life: a series of tech-meets-fun events that make people feel excited to show up, not just for learning, but for the experience. Back in undergrad, I organized a mini event during our tech fest called "Code for a Coin" — a fast-paced coding challenge where participants raced to solve fun logic puzzles in the fastest time possible. The simplicity, energy, and competitiveness drew huge crowds — and what surprised me most was how even non-coders got curious and involved.

I'd love to adapt something like that for Generate — maybe something like “Fastest Finger First” with creative tech prompts, or rapid-fire design/coding pair challenges. The goal isn’t just to test skills, but to build comfort, connections, and joy through the process.

For onboarding, my core principle is simple: listen. With the pace of AI and tools today, many can do the work — but what sets people apart is intent, mindset, and potential. Not everyone is expressive or loud, but that doesn’t mean they’re not passionate. I believe onboarding should focus on observing, asking thoughtful questions, and giving space for people to grow into their voice — not force them to perform it right away.

These aren’t giant ideas - just sparks. But I believe that’s how community is built: one small, intentional step at a time.

column21: I understand the commitment and am certain I can make it.
column23: Generate has been more than just a student organization for me—it’s been a way to get involved in a community of like-minded students and a launchpad for building meaningful, lasting relationships with a large network of students and alumni. As a event coordinator on the Alumni Relations team, I witnessed firsthand how much alumni are willing to give when the outreach is intentional and respectful of their time. One of the most rewarding experiences was organizing an alumni panel last semester that connected 200 students with six professionals--- an event that I hope to bring back and create a series out of. That event captured what Generate does best: it builds bridges between people, ideas, and careers. The opportunity to take those values further—as Chief—feels like a natural next step in what I hope to bring to Generate.
column24: Coaching and coordinating Elementary Science Olympiad for four years fundamentally shaped how I understand leadership, collaboration, and growth. I worked with hundreds of young students and educators across Southern California to build a competitive yet inclusive STEM program. That experience taught me how to foster engagement, adapt communication styles across different demographics, and motivate diverse teams toward a common goal. It also showed me the power of early mentorship—how confidence and interest can spark when someone simply feels seen and supported. At Generate, I’d bring that same focus on accessibility and long-term growth—building alumni programming that meets people where they are and nurtures meaningful, lasting engagement.
column25:
I lead with a people-first mindset. I prioritize open communication, consistent mentorship, and ownership-based delegation. I learned a lot from Seby, the previous Alumni Relations coach, in his ability to organize and lead our team the past semester.

In practice, this means setting clear expectations, sharing context—not just tasks—and regularly checking in on both progress and morale. I also believe recognition builds momentum, so I’m intentional about celebrating small wins and keeping team members motivated to keep putting their best work forward. Whether I’m managing a small team or a larger one, I aim to create a space where people feel supported to experiment, chase their ideas, and grow. Leadership to me isn’t about having all the answers—it’s about creating the conditions where others can feel comfortable to share and try new things.
column26:
Having already served on the Alumni Relations team, I bring a unique understanding of both the logistical challenges and the untapped opportunities within our alumni engagement strategy. I know what it takes to get responses, coordinate schedules, and build meaningful programming that respects alumni time while keeping students excited—and I hope to bring new ideas this semester as well. 

Here are a few initiatives I’d love to implement:

Alumni Speaker Series: A curated set of thematic panels—centered around topics like fintech, product development, or startup pivots—designed to give current members actionable insights while celebrating the diverse expertise in our alumni network. 

Alumni-Only Events: Whether it’s a casual digital reunion, a city-based mixer, or a virtual “un-conference,” I want to create dedicated spaces for alumni to reconnect with one another. These events strengthen bonds, foster inter-alumni mentorship, and remind them that they’re still part of a living community.

Alumni Newsletter: A monthly or bi-monthly digest that highlights job changes, product launches, alumni features, upcoming events, and ways to give back. This keeps the Generate brand top-of-mind and reinforces the sense of belonging, even after graduation.

These ideas are grounded in what I’ve learned from hands-on experience—what works, what doesn’t, and what alumni actually respond to. I’m excited to take that insight and scale it with a strategic, human-first approach.

column21: I understand the commitment and am certain I can make it.
column23:
Hi! I haven’t been a part of GENERATE before, but I’m reaching out now because I believe I can bring a lot of relevant experience to the table. During my undergrad (2019–2023), I spent around 3.5 years leading and managing a similar club as a technical and projects lead.

In addition to that, I served as chairperson of a student team of over 600 members, where I worked closely with five other leads. My main focus was communication, specifically building a system where our team could effectively reach out to alumni and connect them with current students for mentorship and industry exposure.

Beyond my leadership roles, I’ve interned at organizations like ISRO India, worked as an HR-I/O intern, contributed as a web content writer at media, and even published a book. I know that’s quite a mix, but I’d love for you to check out my LinkedIn profile; it gives a clearer picture of why I genuinely feel GENERATE would be the right fit for me.
column24:
One of the many student clubs I was involved with during my undergrad was GUSAC, a student-led organization with a legacy of over 10 years. During my time there, I had the opportunity to collaborate with Red Bull to host one of the biggest carnivals on campus, where we created a platform for hundreds of students to showcase their talents.

I also served as the president of a writers’ club, where we launched the college’s first-ever podcast. While it’s no longer active, leading the team and being part of something new and impactful was an unforgettable experience and something I’m really proud of.
column25:
My approach to leading a team is based on clear communication, empathy, and shared responsibility. I believe in recognizing individual strengths, delegating thoughtfully, and creating a supportive space where everyone feels valued.

I prioritize traits like adaptability, accountability, and trust. To build morale, I make time for regular check-ins, celebrate small wins, and ensure people know their work matters. Leadership, to me, is about guiding while empowering others to lead in their own way.
column26: I have my own way of onboarding people, starting from filtering people out based on mini-tasks I generally give them to come up with solutions. Reaching out to alumni was one of the hardest tasks I had ever taken up, and I still managed to reach out to 70% of them to onboard them on a platform my university was building back then to help and guide current students.
